factors.summary: The supply chain is healthy. The company has implemented automation and efficient distribution strategies, improving fulfillment and lowering freight costs.

evidence.text: To improve the efficiency, accuracy, and capacity of our distribution centers, we made significant investments into distribution automation over the last several years (a majority of our facilities are now automated, and greater than 80% of our picking occurs at an automated distribution center).

factors.summary: Overall demand appears to be decelerating, particularly in the manufacturing and non-residential construction markets.

evidence.text: The first nine months of 2015 were hit hard by a slowdown in our business with customers connected to the oil and gas industry. This connection includes direct industry participants as well as those with a geographic connection.

factors.summary: Productivity is improving through better average net sales per store and efficient management of operating expenses.

evidence.text: Given the weak end markets, this is difficult to predict; therefore, we continued to aggressively manage our operating and administrative expenses during this period. A portion of our operating and administrative expense management is structural as an increase in average net sales per store/selling location leverages our fixed costs (a concept we have historically described as the 'pathway to profit'). Another portion is great people rising to the challenge by reducing other operating and administrative expenses! Simply put, in the last twelve months we funded all of our headcount expansion by spending less everywhere else. A simple concept, but it takes a focused effort to pull it off.

factors.summary: Free cash flow is improving, with strong operating cash flow relative to net earnings and an increase in net cash provided by operating activities. Favorable working capital trends, including decreased cash requirements for net working capital growth, are contributing to this improvement. However, there is a slight offset from the timing of income tax payments.

evidence.text: During the third quarter of 2015, we generated $140,831 (or 103.2% of net earnings) of operating cash flow... Year-to-date, we generated $401,985 (or 99.4% of net earnings) of operating cash flow.

Net cash provided by operating activities increased from the prior year. The increase was driven by growth in net earnings and a decrease in the cash required to fund our net working capital growth, which includes trade accounts receivable and inventory changes. This was partially offset by the timing of income tax payments.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

factors.summary: The company has increased its debt leverage, primarily through borrowings to fund purchases of common stock and dividend payments, which reflects a strategic approach to balancing growth initiatives with shareholder returns. This increase in debt is part of a broader deleveraging strategy, as the company is managing its capital structure to support future growth.

evidence.text: During 2014, and the first nine months of 2015, we incurred some debt to fund capital expenditures, purchases of our common stock, and payments of dividends as further discussed earlier in this document. This was expected and is expected to continue for the remainder of 2015.

Net cash used in financing activities consisted of the payments of dividends and purchases of our common stock, partially offset by the proceeds from the exercise of stock options and borrowings. During the first nine months of 2015 and 2014, we utilized borrowings to fund certain needs including borrowings in the first nine months of 2015 to fund the increased purchases of our common stock.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

factors.summary: The margin trajectory of the company shows mixed trends. While gross profit margins have remained stable or slightly improved in certain periods, there are pressures from economic fluctuations, currency impacts, and competitive dynamics affecting the ability to maintain margins. Additionally, operating and administrative expenses have shown some improvement as a percentage of net sales, but overall, the margins are under varying levels of stress and stability.

evidence.text: Gross and operating margins are showing a mixed trend with gross profit declining slightly over time but operating and administrative expenses improving as a percentage of net sales.

Net cash provided by operating activities increased from the prior year. The increase was driven by growth in net earnings and a decrease in the cash required to fund our net working capital growth, which includes trade accounts receivable and inventory changes. This was partially offset by the timing of income tax payments.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

evidence.text: The biggest impact came from customers engaged in the oil and gas business, but we also experienced weakness in other industries, as evidenced by the decline in the PMI Index.
Our gross profit decreased from 50.8% in the third quarter of 2014 and increased from 50.3% in the second quarter of 2015 to 50.5% in the third quarter of 2015.

factors.summary: The company is effectively managing costs through a combination of structural improvements, optimizing net working capital, controlling capital expenditures, and enhancing employee efficiency. These initiatives have led to a decrease in operating and administrative expenses as a percentage of net sales, increased net cash provided by operating activities, and a focused effort on reducing expenses while funding headcount expansion.

evidence.text: Operating and administrative expenses decreased as a percentage of net sales in both the first nine months and the third quarter of 2015 versus the same periods of 2014.

Net cash provided by operating activities increased from the prior year. The increase was driven by growth in net earnings and a decrease in the cash required to fund our net working capital growth, which includes trade accounts receivable and inventory changes. This was partially offset by the timing of income tax payments.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

evidence.text: A portion of our operating and administrative expense management is structural as an increase in average net sales per store/selling location leverages our fixed costs (a concept we have historically described as the 'pathway to profit'). Another portion is great people rising to the challenge by reducing other operating and administrative expenses! Simply put, in the last twelve months we funded all of our headcount expansion by spending less everywhere else. A simple concept, but it takes a focused effort to pull it off.

factors.summary: The company is consistently increasing shareholder returns through a combination of increased dividends and stock buybacks, which reflects management's confidence in the company's performance and future prospects.

evidence.text: Our dividends (per share basis) were as follows in 2015 and 2014: [table showing increased dividends]. During the first, second, and third quarters of 2015, we purchased 2,000,000, 4,000,000, and 600,000 shares respectively, of our common stock...
Net cash used in financing activities consisted of the payments of dividends and purchases of our common stock, partially offset by the proceeds from the exercise of stock options and borrowings.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

factors.summary: The company is increasing capital expenditures, particularly in industrial vending and distribution automation. This trend is evident from the evidence provided in the SEC filing, which highlights the significant impact of these areas on capital expenditures in recent years.

evidence.text: Cash requirements for property and equipment expenditures were satisfied from net earnings, cash on hand, borrowings, and the proceeds of disposals. We anticipate funding our current expansion plans with cash generated from operations, from available cash and cash equivalents, and from our borrowing capacity.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

evidence.text: Of these factors, the items related to industrial vending created the greatest impact to our capital expenditures in 2015, while the items related to industrial vending and distribution automation created the greatest impact to our capital expenditures in 2014.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

factors.summary: The end markets are experiencing mixed momentum, with the manufacturing sector, particularly the industrial production business, showing signs of weakness. The non-residential construction market is also facing challenges, and there is a notable slowdown in the oil and gas sector affecting both direct industry participants and geographically connected businesses.

evidence.text: The company's end markets are experiencing mixed momentum. The manufacturing sector, particularly the industrial production business, is showing signs of weakness, while the maintenance portion of manufacturing and non-residential construction are more stable but also facing challenges.

Fluctuations in end market business – The sequential trends noted above were directly linked to fluctuations in our end markets. Approximately 50% of our business is with customers engaged in manufacturing. The daily sales to these customers grew, when compared to the same period in the prior year, as follows: [Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Annual percentages provided].

The first nine months of 2015 were hit hard by a slowdown in our business with customers connected to the oil and gas industry. This connection includes direct industry participants as well as those with a geographic connection.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth has shown signs of deceleration. While net cash provided by operating activities has increased, the company's sales growth at stores opened greater than five years has declined, and net sales growth has significantly decreased from 14.3% in 2014 to 1.5% in 2015.

evidence.text: Net cash provided by operating activities increased from the prior year. The increase was driven by growth in net earnings and a decrease in the cash required to fund our net working capital growth, which includes trade accounts receivable and inventory changes. This was partially offset by the timing of income tax payments.
evidence.subsection: CRITICAL ACCOUNTING POLICIES / LIQUIDITY AND CAPITAL RESOURCES

evidence.text: During the months noted below, the stores opened greater than five years had daily sales growth rates of (compared to the same month in the preceding year): [2015, 2014, 2013 growth rates provided]. This group, which represented about 90% of our total sales in the first nine months of 2015, is more cyclical due to the increased market share they enjoy in their local markets.
Net sales percentage change was 5.0% for the nine-month period in 2015 compared to 11.7% in 2014.

factors.summary: The company is experiencing growth in unit volumes or transaction counts, particularly in its non-fastener product lines, which account for about 60% of sales. However, the growth rate has slowed compared to previous periods, indicating a shift towards higher volume but not necessarily higher-margin segments. The non-fastener business has shown resilience but was still affected by a weak industrial environment.

evidence.text: The non-fastener business demonstrated greater relative resilience over the last several years, when compared to our fastener business and to the distribution industry in general, due to our strong FAST Solutions® (industrial vending) program. However, this business was not immune to the impact of a weak industrial environment.

The increase in net sales in the periods noted for 2015 and 2014 came primarily from higher unit sales. The higher unit sales resulted primarily from increases in sales at older store locations and to a lesser degree the opening of new store locations in the last several years.

factors.summary: The company is facing challenges in maintaining pricing power, with evidence of stable pricing but margin pressure from economic fluctuations and currency impacts, particularly in foreign countries. Additionally, there is a noted price deflation in the fastener product line, indicating further pricing pressures.

evidence.text: The change in currencies in foreign countries (primarily Canada) relative to the United States dollar impacted our net sales growth over the last several years. During the years 2013 and 2014, it lowered our net sales growth by 0.2% and 0.5%, respectively. In the first nine months of 2015, it lowered our net sales growth by 1.1%.

the sale of production fasteners is also a sticky business in the short-term as it is expensive and time consuming for our customers to change their supplier relationships. While our customer base values the capabilities we bring to the table, in the last nine months this group of customers has seen a contraction in its production and therefore its need for fasteners.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting towards non-fastener products, particularly industrial vending and maintenance solutions, which now account for about 60% of sales. This shift is driven by the company's FAST Solutions® program, which has demonstrated greater resilience compared to the fastener business and the broader distribution industry. However, while the non-fastener segment is more resilient, it is not necessarily higher-margin, and the fastener product line has experienced contraction, contributing to a shift towards lower-margin segments.

evidence.text: The non-fastener business demonstrated greater relative resilience over the last several years, when compared to our fastener business and to the distribution industry in general, due to our strong FAST Solutions® (industrial vending) program.

Our growth in net sales was not meaningfully impacted by the introduction of new products or services, with one exception. Over the last several years, our FAST Solutions ® (industrial vending) initiative has stimulated faster growth with a subset of our customers.

factors.summary: The competitive environment is intensifying, with the company facing challenges from competitors, particularly in the fastener business. Customers are increasingly preferring to reduce the number of suppliers and utilize technology to improve availability and reduce waste.

evidence.text: The non-fastener business demonstrated greater relative resilience over the last several years, when compared to our fastener business and to the distribution industry in general, due to our strong FAST Solutions® (industrial vending) program.
Many customers would prefer to reduce their number of suppliers to simplify their business, and many customers would prefer to utilize various technologies to improve availability and reduce waste.

factors.summary: The company faces risks due to significant customer concentration, particularly in the manufacturing and construction sectors, which account for approximately 50% of its business. Additionally, the top 100 national account customers represent about 24% of sales, with mixed performance observed across different customer groups.

evidence.text: Approximately 50% of our business has historically been with customers engaged in some type of manufacturing. The daily sales to these customers grew, when compared to the same period in the prior year, as follows: [Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Annual percentages provided].

The business with our top 100 national account customers (representing approximately 24% of sales) experienced poor sales results in the third quarter of 2015, with net sales growth of approximately 1%, while sales to our remaining national account customers (representing approximately 22% of sales) grew approximately 13%.

factors.summary: The company's competitive advantages are strengthening, with the FAST Solutions® program highlighting resilience in the non-fastener segment. Additionally, the company emphasizes a strong local presence, efficient supply chain, and comprehensive customer service as key differentiators that enhance its ability to grow by understanding and addressing customer needs effectively.

evidence.text: The non-fastener business demonstrated greater relative resilience over the last several years, when compared to our fastener business and to the distribution industry in general, due to our strong FAST Solutions® (industrial vending) program.

We believe our ability to grow is amplified if we can service our customers at the closest economic point of contact. For us, this 'closest economic point of contact' is the local store; therefore, our focus centers on understanding our customers' day, their opportunities, and their obstacles.

factors.summary: Management has maintained consistent forward guidance, signaling confidence in the company's growth prospects through planned store openings and investments in industrial vending solutions.

evidence.text: Our preliminary estimate for 2016 is to open 60 to 75 stores. [...] We opened 13 stores in the first nine months of 2015, and currently expect to open approximately 30 stores in total for 2015.
We plan to expand on these themes, as well as our current merchandising rollout, during our next Investor Day, which is planned for the fourth quarter of 2015.

factors.summary: Input costs appear to be stable overall, with minimal price changes in non-fastener products and some deflation in fastener products. However, economic fluctuations, particularly currency changes in foreign countries, have impacted net sales growth negatively in recent years.

evidence.text: The change in currencies in foreign countries (primarily Canada) relative to the United States dollar impacted our net sales growth over the last several years. During the years 2013 and 2014, it lowered our net sales growth by 0.2% and 0.5%, respectively. In the first nine months of 2015, it lowered our net sales growth by 1.1%.
Net sales were also impacted by minimal price changes in our non-fastener products and some price deflation in our fastener products, but the net impact was a drag on growth.

factors.summary: Labor costs are being managed through various strategies, including store consolidation, focused expense reduction, and efficient hour management. While there is no mention of significant wage inflation or margin pressure, the company has shown a proactive approach in managing labor-related expenses.

evidence.text: We have recorded the impaired future costs related to these commitments. The related expense was not material as these locations have relatively short lease commitments and minimal leasehold improvements.

We added 1,554 people to the Fastenal organization in the last twelve months (about 83% of these people were added to a store or some other type of selling location), and a large majority of these additions came in the last nine months. This provided a meaningful increase in our capacity. However, we needed to fund this increased capacity. We did this by (1) reducing our total operating and administrative expenses outside of payroll related costs, and (2) managing our hours worked in a very focused site by site fashion.

factors.summary: Management is cautiously optimistic about the company's growth initiatives, such as industrial vending and expanding the distribution network. Despite investing aggressively and believing in their first mover advantage, they remain cautious about economic conditions.

evidence.text: We believe industrial vending will be an important chapter in the Fastenal story; we also believe it has the potential to be transformative to industrial distribution, and that we have a 'first mover' advantage. Given this, we have been investing aggressively to maximize the advantage.

We are a growth focused organization and we constantly strive to make investments into the growth drivers of our business. These investments typically center on people. By adding more people we add to our ability to interact with and 'to serve' our customers from our local store and to 'back them up' in some type of support role.

factors.summary: The near-term risks are predominantly negative, with significant challenges in the oil and gas sector and construction industry, a strong U.S. dollar impacting sales, and weak sequential sales patterns in end markets.

evidence.text: The biggest impact came from customers engaged in the oil and gas business, but we also experienced weakness in other industries, as evidenced by the decline in the PMI Index.

Negative – (1) The first nine months of 2015 were hit hard by a slowdown in our business with customers connected to the oil and gas industry. This connection includes direct industry participants as well as those with a geographic connection. (2) The first nine months of 2015 were negatively affected by a strong U.S. dollar, relative to other currencies, which hurts our U.S. customer base (which accounts for approximately 89% of sales). (3) Our end markets remain choppy, as demonstrated by our weak sequential patterns.

factors.summary: The company is sensitive to economic conditions, particularly in manufacturing and construction sectors, and currency fluctuations. These factors are key variables impacting near-term results.

evidence.text: The change in currencies in foreign countries (primarily Canada) relative to the United States dollar impacted our net sales growth over the last several years. During the years 2013 and 2014, it lowered our net sales growth by 0.2% and 0.5%, respectively. In the first nine months of 2015, it lowered our net sales growth by 1.1%.
The impact of currency fluctuations (primarily Canada) relative to the United States dollar is noted in the table above.

factors.summary: The company has a clear and well-resourced long-term strategic direction focused on expanding its industrial vending operations, diversifying product lines, and increasing store locations. The strategy emphasizes a 'first mover' advantage in industrial vending and includes aggressive investments to maintain this position. The company plans to discuss these initiatives further during its next Investor Day in the fourth quarter of 2015.

evidence.text: The company's long-term strategic direction is clear and well-resourced. It is focused on expanding its industrial vending operations and diversifying its product lines to enhance growth and resilience.

We believe industrial vending will be an important chapter in the Fastenal story; we also believe it has the potential to be transformative to industrial distribution, and that we have a 'first mover' advantage. Given this, we have been investing aggressively to maximize the advantage.
We plan to expand on these themes, as well as our current merchandising rollout, during our next Investor Day, which is planned for the fourth quarter of 2015.

factors.summary: The company identifies two key catalysts: the expansion of the industrial vending program, which is expected to enhance customer retention and sales efficiency, and the upcoming Investor Day in the fourth quarter of 2015, aimed at discussing growth initiatives like merchandising. These initiatives reflect the company's strategic focus on growth and innovation.

evidence.text: We believe industrial vending will be an important chapter in the Fastenal story; we also believe it has the potential to be transformative to industrial distribution, and that we have a 'first mover' advantage. Given this, we have been investing aggressively to maximize the advantage.
We plan to expand on these themes, as well as our current merchandising rollout, during our next Investor Day, which is planned for the fourth quarter of 2015.

factors.summary: Capacity utilization is increasing, driven by the expansion of the distribution network, new facilities, store openings, and an increase in employee count. The company is investing in a high frequency distribution center (T-HUB) and plans to open 60 to 75 new stores in 2016, reflecting a strategic focus on growth.

evidence.text: We also added a high frequency distribution center, internally known as T-HUB, to support vending and other high frequency selling activities.
We plan to expand our pace of store openings in 2016 with a goal of opening 60 to 75 new stores (an increase of approximately 2% to 3% over our number of stores as of September 30, 2015).

factors.summary: The company is effectively managing inventory levels through strategic placement and tight control, which has led to reduced excess stock, lower freight costs, and improved fulfillment. Despite weaker net sales growth, this efficient management has minimized the impact of supplier incentives.

evidence.text: These strategies allowed us to better target where to stock certain products (local store, regional distribution center, master stocking hub, or supplier) and allowed us to improve our fulfillment, lower our freight costs, and improve our ability to serve a broader range of customers.
With weaker net sales growth and our tight management of inventory levels, the growth of spending with our suppliers is lower; hence, our supplier incentives are reduced.

factors.summary: The company is maintaining market share and gaining in certain areas. Despite economic challenges and a slowdown in market growth, the company's strong FAST Solutions® program and customer relationships have helped preserve its position. The non-fastener product line has shown resilience and continued market share gains, with growth outperforming both its end markets and competitors, though at a slower pace than previous periods.

evidence.text: The non-fastener business demonstrated greater relative resilience over the last several years, when compared to our fastener business and to the distribution industry in general, due to our strong FAST Solutions® (industrial vending) program.

The non-fastener product line has seen its daily sales growth decrease from about 18% growth in the last six months of 2014 to about 6% growth in the third quarter of 2015. The weak environment has hurt our business, but the 6% growth compared to our end markets and to our competitors continues to demonstrate strong market share gains.
